tokens: (Joking) You never let me look outside at all.
Hey, you can see the boulder tracks (on the North Massif)!
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're standing by for your Go.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You're looking great here so far.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: There are boulders all over those massifs.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We could have gone all around and looked around.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We should have hovered around a little bit; gone and looked at the Scarp.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Jack laughs) I like it right where we are.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: While you're waiting on that Go, I shot for a spot, around 2 o'clock from Poppie.
There's a number of boulders out at 12 o'clock from Poppie, and I really think I'm probably not more than about 100 meters out in front of it, and slightly to the north.
Actually, I may be a little bit closer to Trident than I expected Poppie to be.
I think I've got Trident right out the left window.
And our first cut at the mobility around here in the Rover (is that) it ought to be super.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: But I tell you, the Massifs and Bear Mountain are two different products.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) Of course, they're different slopes, too.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think you're looking, probably...That may be Rudolph, right there, Jack, out your window.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I was looking more at those boulders and trying to stay in the spots in between them than I was...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Man, there was practically no dust, just a little bit of a film.
You had the ground all the way to the ground.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I could call touchdown on the shadow.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're hanging in for your Go.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Relishing the thought of doing geology at this magnificent site) It better be a Go!
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You know, you can't see into Camelot, Jack; that rim is Camelot out in front of us.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Challenger, you'll be glad to hear you're Stay for T-1.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordy, you're a smooth talker, you know it?
You can forget all I told you about Verb 22 Noun 46.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's find out where we are (in the checklist).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I need a P12 time as soon as I get (V)60...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Copy that down, Jack, right here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: 20:21 and 30:75, and I'm going to P12.
I need a P12 time from you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I can't feel any difference between one-sixth g and anything else right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) I got to change these numbers.
(Pause) You didn't get an update on Noun 76, did you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How would you like me to handle R-3 of Noun 76?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How was the view on the way down, Gene?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You know, after we pitched over, I was just looking for a place to land, (so) I'm not sure.
I just didn't want to hit one of those boulders out there which would have been as easy...And look at that.
Look at right in front of us.
I didn't want to land there either.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I see that one right in front of us.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: R-3, cross range, is okay as is.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We're in posture for a T-2, Gordy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Good thing we didn't plan to go to Hanover.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Look at the boulder, halfway up the hill.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, the boulder tracks; they're beautiful.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's sitting right there in the end of the track.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There are tracks all over that hillside.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: There's a boulder came right down to the surface there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That one (a boulder track) went right through that little crater...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...(and it's) sitting right there for us to sample.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll bet Bear Mountain and the Sculptured Hills are the same.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll have to look at it from outside.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Now I see why they call them "sculptured".
My god, they're so hummocky that there's shadow all over them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: God, there are some holes and rocks around here.
Who told me this was a flat landing site?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's see, we got about 2 degrees left (roll) and about 5 degrees pitchup.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Our (LM) shadow's about 100 feet (long), Geno, I think.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, we're only about...Yeah, less than 100 meters then.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It doesn't look that long, but it is...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: God, there are some holes I'm glad I didn't land in around here, I'll tell you.
(Pause) Now, if you look at the (South) Massif, Jack.
I don't know if you can see it over here.
You see...They're almost like a series of linear boulder tracks, but they come crossways down the slope.
So it looks like there may very definitely be some jointed...There's outcrop on top the Massif, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, it sure looks like it: gray outcrop.
It's a bluish gray compared to the brown- or tan-gray of the massif side.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And a lot of that outcrop down on the bottom is boulder.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Do you know what that reminds me of, way up on top...that outcrop?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) Gordy, I noticed something ever since we've landed.
The oxidizer quantity went from 7 or 8, and now it's down to 2, and the fuel has stayed constant.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) I noticed the Quantity light also.
I was thinking "Reg light", though, when I saw it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Challenger, we'll have a story on that (quantity light) for you later.
We don't think we were really low level.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: It doesn't make any difference now, Gordy; except to talk about when we get home.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And we're 2 minutes and counting to T-2.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We better hurry if they're going to give us the Go.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's something everyone's got to do once in their life.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're not going to have much time for T-2...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You're Stay for T-2, and Go for the DPS (Descent Propulsion System, pronounced "dips") vent.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Stay for T-2, and Go for the DPS (pronounced "dips") vent.
Let me get out of...Okay, we can't hack that.
I'm going to get out of P12.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can unzap that water, if you'd like.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Regulator 1 is Closed, Houston; Oxidizer (and) Fuel Vents coming Open.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We did not hear anything on that one, Gordy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Reading from the checklist as they reconfigure the LM) Okay.
We'll monitor oxidizer pressure until 20 to 40 (psi), and then Ox Vent, Closed; fuel pressure to less than 8 (psi).
(Pause) Descent Quantity light...(correcting himself) Reg light.
Mode Control, two to Att Hold.
Well, we just keep going, I guess.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Gene is now reading from the checklist as Jack sets controls on his side of the spacecraft) Okay.
Battery 5 is Off/Reset, and it's off the line.
Battery 6, Off/Reset, and it's off the line.
(Pause) CWEA (Caution and Warning Electronics Assembly, pronounced "seewee") cycling, cycled; both lights are out.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And then, (Pressure Regulators) A and B going to Cabin.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Take your helmet and gloves off.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm using it instead of a light switch because I've got it covered up.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack's going to pick up the
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Your DPS Oxidizer Pressure is 40 (psi) or less.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: (Pause) Gordy, while the P57 is doing its gravity work, let me say that the L&A (see below) and the landing site, from a relief point of view, I think, are identical.
I couldn't say enough for the L&A.
I actually didn't look around nearly as much as I thought I would, or as I wanted to, because I had fixation on a reasonable spot to land.
They're not all reasonable in that there's some very subtle, hummocky-like craters right in and around where we are.
And there's not a lot of boulders laying on the surface, but there's a lot of what appear to be boulders that are covered up by some of the dark mantle.
Numerous enough that you would not like to take a chance at putting a pad down on one of them or in one of those hummocky, subtle craters.
As a result, I really didn't have a chance to look all around at where I wanted to except to put the bird down where I wanted it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got no complaint with that.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I guess the thing that probably surprised me most about the site, as far as landing is concerned, is the fact that there were these buried, massive pieces of rock out here in the plains area, partially covered and filleted by the dark mantle.
I hesitate to say they're outcrops but certainly they're buried massive pieces of rock (and we'll have to wait to find out) whether they're boulders or not.
I expected to find a number of craters, but I guess I really didn't expect to find the rock types (that is, buried boulders) around.
And we're talking about anywhere from 1 to 2 meters down to, oh, 2 or 3 feet, which, when they're sticking out and on the sides of some of these subtle craters, look pretty menacing.
But that probably is the one thing that surprised me most.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The visibility prior to pitchover was such that I could see Nansen.
I could not see Camelot until after pitchover.
However, once I had pitchover, if I could have froze(n) it right there like we do in the
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me say that the inside of the spacecraft looked just like the simulator.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: All the way through PDI prior to pitchover, Jack and I had the real America - or the other America - right smack out the front window all the way down, which was pretty spectacular.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And you can consider yourself Stay for T-3.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: You're getting smoother (that is, becoming a smoother talker) all the time.
If you're happy with Noun 22, I'll Pro(ceed).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I had the angles matched on the steerable high-gain antenna and went to Slew, and it (the signal strength) held for a few seconds and then dropped off.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It looks pretty good (his tone of voice suggests "but not optimal").
You might try to peak it up just a little more.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: No, we're on an omni(-directional antenna) now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'll try the steerable one more time here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're on the steerable, and I'm not going to touch it.
(Pause) It's Steerable and it's Slew, and I got 3.8 (carrier signal strength).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That looks good to us, Jack.
(Long Pause) Jack, we'd like you to verify the tape recorder, Off.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordy, how does the fuel vent look to you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can go ahead and close it.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordy, I guess I'm puzzled on that one.
(Pause) You see anything we did wrong?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene, our only guess is that you might have loaded Noun 88 wrong.
We'd like you to start over, and we'll watch you real close again.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Listen, I think we know what we did.
We loaded Spiral for Cursor, and Cursor for Spiral.
How would it be if we went through the P57 again and...(Pause) I guess we got to do it all over.
Those old numbers are no good anymore.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm sure that's what we did.
We loaded Cursor for Spiral, and Spiral for Cursor.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gordy, you going to want a recycle on this gravity measurement?
I doubt if it (the computer) will need it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) No; no recycle necessary this time through.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordy, let me comment about the handling of the bird.
After you once fly it (the LM) around in orbit a little bit, you get accustomed to the thrusters, and it came back to me quite a bit from (Apollo) 10, anyway.
And you get a feel for acceleration and deceleration as well as the attitude-hold capability.
And, really, the response, even with a heavy descent stage near the surface, is phenomenal.
Responded exactly in the direction I wanted, held attitude very good.
And, let me tell you, the
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll go ahead and torque them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're standing by for the (computer memory) dumps.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I'm ready to give the E-mem...(Hearing Fullerton) Coming at you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gordy, one other thing about the landing.
I saw the (Contact) light, I think.
And I heard Jack call it, the Contact light.
I think I waited about a second and hit the Stop button.
And, of course, you could feel the fall.
I don't really feel we fell that much, but it was quite a change in acceleration at that point.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I guess I had, from what I would guess, a foot or two per second forward speed on that one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And let me know when I can have the computer, please.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I'm standing by with parking angles when you're ready to load them.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We'll be ready in a second.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: These are the IMU parking angles.
(Pause) I see you loading the radar.
Do you just want to load these or write them down?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Y will be plus all zeros.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Noun 20 will be plus 295.86, plus all zeros, plus 084.14.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm going to power down the
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'd like you to read out 047 and 053 to us.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: If you're happy with Noun 22, I'll Enter them.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And it just dawned on me.
I'm sorry about the zero on the Noun 69.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Am I Go to pull the (AGS circuit) breaker?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're happy with Noun 20, and you're clear to power down the AGS.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Gordy, the breakers are coming Open on
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gordo, the PLSS is against the hatch, and we're installing the BRA.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gordy, we're not going any further, and we'll answer your questions here when we get some time.
My best guess is 150 meters from Poppie at 1 to 2 o'clock.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, Houston, we're just starting our eat period.
Sorry to be a little behind.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're starting to cut into a little lunch here and, if you've got any questions, why don't you come up with them now?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're wondering if you can give us estimate of the angular position - clock position - of Rudolph.
And can you line up Rudolph with a horizontal feature out beyond it?
(Pause) I should say "horizon feature" - out in the distance - not "horizontal".
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Houston, I have calmed down, but be advised that our dinner is corn chowder.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: He went to Captain's Mast for (flatulence from) eating that the other day.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston, how do you read - (Correcting himself) "Challenger" - or whoever we are?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: "Whoever you are", you're loud and clear.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I took the monocular and looked at some large boulders at our 12 o'clock position.
They're probably on the order of a half meter to 2 meters, buried but without strong filleting.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And most of them that I could see had the same mottled light-gray and medium-gray texture, and it looked like there's a lineation in it.
And whatever the mottling is, it's on a grain size or fragment size of a few centimeters, and it looks as if it's very uniform in that mottling; that is, there's one fragment size.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: There are a few (boulders) near one crater out at 12 o'clock (that are) dark-gray rock that may be glass coated.
Matter of fact, one of them looks like it's right at the rim and might have been part of a projectile that made the crater.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: The large boulder that I mentioned that's several meters in diameter...I'm not even sure it's a boulder.
It does have a well-developed fillet.
It looks like the fractures generally are north-south.
At least we can't see end on into the fractures.
And it's too far away to be sure, but it looks like it's mottled also, although there did appear in the monocular to be a more heterogeneous mottling.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That boulder ought to be very close to the ALSEP site.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (This paragraph has been edited for clarity) Gordo, in reference to these boulders, everywhere I can see out of my left window and out ahead of me - in referring to that boulder (Geophone Rock) Jack's talking about which is just a little bit on my side at 12 o'clock - it appears that the dark mantle has filleted and, for the most part, has covered part of or is up on top of some of the crevices and the crannies in the boulders themselves, with the exception of - well, I'll take that back - even the very small ones.

I'd say (that) boulders of the size Jack's talking about - (boulders of a size) anywhere from 1 to 2 to 3 meters - that are visible through the surface, (cover) a very small percentage (of the surface), but when you look at them at our level, it looks like they are quite populous.
I'd say there are maybe about 25 of them in view between myself and where the horizon falls off down away from us towards the South Massif (slightly south of west).

The area back towards Station 1, at least the other side of Trident (off to the south and southeast), looks like it's more heavily strewn with some of these filleted and partially-mantled large fragments.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: To say that there is a boulder, as such, actually sitting on the surface, I really can't find one, unless they're around some of these very small and possibly younger craters.
But I think for the most part everything is somewhat mantled.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gordy, I think maybe the predictions of a fairly thin regolith (the soil layer) were good.
I have a crater at about, oh, 130 feet (from the LM).
It looks like it's not more than a meter deep.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's very fresh, has a bright halo around it, and it's very rocky in its interior and has some rocks that are at least 10 or 20 centimeters in diameter on the rim.
It looks like it's penetrated into some much rockier substrate than what we're seeing on the surface.
The surface itself looks like (it has), oh, probably 15 percent (coverage by) fragments greater than half a centimeter.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I don't see any general size sorting.
I do have a crater out here that's maybe a meter in diameter - fairly fresh, although not bright halo(ed) - that has not penetrated to blocky material.
And it looks like that the saturation crater size is very small in the area we can see; that is, there don't seem to be any old or very subdued craters.
(Pause) Well, let me think about how to put that again.
It's obviously saturated with craters a few centimeters in diameter, but when you get bigger than that, there seems to be more of a clear distribution rather than a saturation.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Gordy, let me give you a quick (description of the) "far horizon".
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It sure is white, but it's varied shades of white with sort of a tendency on its southern or southeastern slope to sort of be marble caked with a darker material much the same color as the mantle that we've landed on.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There are several places where you can see what appear to be outcrops.
I say "several" - about a dozen anyway - where you can see relatively large areas of outcrop on the South Massif.
That outcrop is of a darker gray color than the white-gray of the Massif itself.
The one most dominant outcrop is right at the change in slope to the west, where it goes upslope and then plateaus off, and there's a definite outcrop.
And you can see several boulders on all levels of the Massif that have come apparently from outcrops and I feel certain we will be able to get to some of those that have come all the way down.

South Massif, too, appears to be, in areas, marbly caked dirty, such as if it was sprinkled with a dirty or a darker covering, and that covering is more evident as it slopes back (down) here towards the east.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: As (for) the far horizon now, I can see the South Massif all the way to 9 o'clock, but then behind it, there's just a little breadloaf-type dome of a much darker, much-more-hummocky mound back there, relatively big.
It's probably, from where I stand, at least 10 percent the size of the South Massif.
There appear to be some lineations running...Well, as I'm looking at them, they're dipping down into the west at about 20 degrees, but that may be a Sun-angle problem.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And then, contrasting that is Bear Mountain which is also much darker gray, much different than the Massif from where I stand, much more hummocky surface.
It appears to be to me what I would expect Sculptured Hills to be like.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: One other thing about the South Massif is that - as I look at it at about 9:30 to 10:30 - there is a little knob of the South Massif that sort of flows towards the east or slightly towards the northeast.
That's the one that tends to be a little bit more heavily covered with the darker, dusty material...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're about 12 (to) 13 minutes behind the timeline for starting cabin preps.
And (the) Backroom is enjoying your descriptions, but we think we'd rather you press on with the preps and get ready to get out for a really good view.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: (Talking faster) We're doing this and eating too.
We're trying to do them both at the same time, and we are pressing.
Just want to say one other thing about the Massif.

I can see a couple of places where very small craters have penetrated the Massif - craters maybe a meter or 2 in size, some (of them) 5 meters - and there's a lot of rock debris around them, which tends to (make me) believe that there is very little, if any, soft covering on that Massif.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gordy, just a couple more words about the North Massif.
It looks like (there is) a good distribution of boulder tracks.
Many of the boulders are accessible.
The tracks can be traced up at least to midslope.
And occasionally, at that midslope position, particularly northwest of Henson, you can see abundant boulders suggestive of outcrop.
That's something that we had missed seeing on the pre-mission photos.
And it isn't as abundant as on the South Massif, but there are apparent ledge formers about midslope.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: LM Crew: (Occasionally keying their microphones by accident) Yeah, let's make (garbled) I don't know (garbled) a lot of (garbled).
(Pause) Give me that to throw away.
transcript.speaker: Unknown

tokens: There's also a few very bright sparkles from the surface - not abundant, but a few.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Unintentional key) Well, let me...I need these.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm going to hand you over to the good Dr.
Have a good trip outside there.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: You do outstanding work and we sure do appreciate it, babe.
transcript.speaker: Cernan
